Digital Learning Resources List: The following list contains digital education resources and tools that can be used to provide continuous learning during the COVID-19 response. Please also note this is simply a curated list; we haven’t vetted this list but do highly recommend considering the highlighted resources, many of which are also available offline, and for low-bandwidth use. This list can be filtered by level, area (STEM, Literacy, or other), and by language. Yellow indicates content we've directly used in low-resource contexts. We have included licensing information where available.

Big Bad Boo produces children’s media that leverages entertainment as a tool to engage and teach children. We are the producers of 1001 Nights, an animated television series that airs in 80 countries and 25 languages on premier channels like Disney, Cartoon Network, Al Jazeera Children’s Channel. It’s one of the top 2 children’s programs on television in a majority of the countries it airs. While uproariously funny, the cartoon also teaches civic education lessons like non-violence, empathy, diversity and other citizenship values. Big Bad Boo just signed a partnerships with UNICEF Jordan to provide refugee children with animated episodes, 500,000 illustrated books, and 50,000 activity books for refugee children in Jordan to support their Makani initiative that provides services to refugee children that cannot access formal education. The 1001 Nights program will provide psychosocial support in these centers. The total value of this program is roughly $500,000.

BluPoint delivers vital information and services to offline and off-grid remote communities.

BluPoint's patent-pending offline Internet solution comprises cloud based services, content conversion software and resilient low energy servers. BluPoint uniquely enables our customers to distribute their content to all mobile devices (including radios and basic phones), incredibly quickly and on-demand, without data charges to the end user. BluPoint overcomes the barriers of no electricity, no Internet, high costs of data and uniquely leverages the high prevalence of basic mobile devices accounting for 75% of handsets in Sub-Saharan Africa in 2015. We do this by automatically adapting the content and making it available within the location via Bluetooth, FM radio, and Wi-Fi.

The BluPoint solution is founded on five key principles:

The content will work on devices that people have in their pockets. Even devices which don’t support Internet connectivity. Today, in sub-Saharan Africa seventy-seven percent of mobile devices are basic phones, unable to browse the internet. BluPoint bridges this digital divide. It allows even basic phones to display digital content.
BluPoint will work even in areas where the Internet doesn’t exist. BluPoint takes a slice of the Internet, as chosen by our customers, and offline it onto BluPoint hubs. Deployed as a single hub, or a collection of hubs that form a wireless network.
BluPoint hubs will work in areas with intermittent or no electricity. The efficient hubs can be solar powered or run off other renewable sources. A car battery will even power a hub. This enables easy mobility, hubs can travel to the remotest communities.
The digital content on the hub will be completely free at the point of use. Anyone can access, browse and download the content with no charge. Removing barriers caused by expensive data and broadband. BluPoint hubs are faster than mobile providers, delivering multimedia content faster than 4G.
Content on the hubs is safe, secure and focused. BluPoint hubs deliver a walled garden of content, focused to the needs of the community. This increase’s focus with limited resources to browse. Users will not be subject to harmful content or marketing agendas.

The American Archive of Public Broadcasting (AAPB) is a collaboration between the Library of Congress and the WGBH Educational Foundation to coordinate a national effort to preserve at-risk public media before its content is lost to posterity and provide a central web portal for access to the unique programming that public stations have aired over the past 70 years. To date, over 110,000 digital files of television and radio programming contributed by more than 130 public media organizations and archives across the United States have been preserved and made accessible for long-term preservation and access. The entire collection is available on location at the Library of Congress and WGBH, and more than 52,000 files are available online at americanarchive.org.

For the first two years, students study online using open source MOOC courses that are already online and established from universities such as Harvard, Duke, etc. The courses done in the first two years are agreed upon by the partner university. The students spend their third year in the partner university and get their degree from this institution. So far, the partner universities are in Germany, but they are speaking to universities in the UK (Russel Group and red brick universities), Paris (Sciences Po) and in Spain. The students are also given the opportunity to do an internship during their studies. The students have language support throughout the course as necessary and in fact Kiron has an agreement with TOEFL for all students to sit the exam for free, so that they also have a language qualification. Those studying in Germany who would like to remain and work, are supported by language courses and are required to have an B1 equivalent level upon graduation and before they can be employed. With their model, the students do not pay anything. However, when they are employed, their employer pays 5% of their wage to Kiron (not sure how long this happens for). No repayment is made until the students are employed. There are currently 1350 students enrolled with Kiron. The projection for the next year is 5000 for April intake and 5000 students for the October intake. By 2020 they would like to have 20 000 students. The value of the project/scholarship per student is 1200Euros. Regarding the set up of the fund it basically works as big pot where private investor provides funds, which is then matched by the government. The fund basically works as big pot where private investor provides money, which is then matched by the German government. The fund will be structured according to the needs of ministry budget logic as well as the compliance needs, and the advisory board of the fund will be able to invest in social entrepreneurial ideas with the object of providing education and higher education with a strong focus on migration and refugees.

In 2015, TidWit and its partners, Mercy Corp and Save the Children, committed to providing a fully digitized Lebanese curriculum for 50,000 Lebanese public school students and Syrian refugees in response to the massive educational challenges posed by the Syrian crisis. TidWit will offer accelerated and remedial material online for free through the public school system, schools for Syrian refugees, community centers, and self-study opportunities. As a result, over a one year period, students will re-enter the school system, obtain non-formal certifications and diplomas necessary for employment, as well as prepare for testing. The pilot developed by TidWiT Inc. provides open access to the Lebanese curriculum to all Lebanese students as well as Syrian refugee students – in 3 languages: English, Arabic and French. The classes are all made available via the TidWiT Online Learning Platform, fully customized to MEHE's look and feel. And it not only provides access to the curriculum but also to supporting material and libraries such as the Khan Academy, Music Libraries, Classic Book libraries, Advanced Concept Libraries and more.

Massive Online Open Course Platfrom The university has opened up space for the nearly 500 Syrian refugee students through the Emergency Refugee Assistance Program. The University is also currently in the planning stages of replicating an Arabized program that would better serve Syrian and other Arab refugees who do not have sufficient English language skills to enroll in its current programs. Students will be expected to learn English over the course of the first three years with the goal of enrolling in courses conducted in English in their final year. This will allow them to engage in discussions with students from multicultural backgrounds in their final year of study. The program intends to offer 10,000 students placements over a 10-year period, provided funding for the initiative can be secured. In addition, students will be supported by Syrian staff, who will ease their transition and guide them throughout the online educational experience. This model will simultaneously provide opportunities for Syrian students and adults seeking employment.

The Self-Learning Programme includes UNRWA TV educational lessons broadcast from Gaza, focusing on Arabic, English, Maths and Science for all students in Grades 4-9. The multimedia episodes have suggestions for activities to do at home with minimal materials, and prompt students to think creatively about the subjects. The episodes are based on the core concepts and skills from the Jordan, Palestinian, Lebanese and Syrian curricula, and so are ideal for any students in these countries or children displaced from these countries. UNRWA TV can be accessed by Palestine refugee children everywhere via NileSat TV (satellite channel 11315 V) or through its dedicated YouTube channel. Although designed for use by Palestine refugees, the materials can be used by any Arabic native speakers who would benefit from educational TV episodes, and the English episodes can be used by any English learners.

Through storytelling, the arts, and serious games, Unsilence creates highly-interactive, research-based learning experiences that help communities learn about social injustice, human rights, and genocide. Our experiences help learners bridge perspectives, break down ingrained stigmas, and build collective empathy and understanding. Unsilence is a 501(c)(3) nonprofit organization.  Unsilence's online interactive features are FREE to use and include choose-your-own-pathway stories, webquests, human rights poetry and stories, interactive exhibitions, and companion education resources.

Pilot project - science/maths/Arabic content via mobile phones. They plan a small scale intervention in Jordan (via the King's sister's foundation) to test the concept.  The basic cost is the SIM card - they work on the basis that people will use their existing 'phones. A partnership with MBRF that will enable the creation of lesson packs for Grade 1-12 in Arabic for Maths and Science and enhancements to the application. All content created will be under an open license (Creative Commons by Attribution ShareAlike) that allows anyone to freely download content to any device and even create their own versions and improvements. By avoiding any need to purchase new devices we are aiming to keep the marginal cost per student for content delivery around the $1-$2 USD per year. The attendance tracking and monitoring functionality is a new addition to the app we are piloting as mentioned with an NGO partner (Study Hall Foundation who have 30 years of experience there). With this system teachers simply use a marker pen on a piece of (possibly laminated) paper to fill in student attendance and then they use the app to take a picture of the sheet. The bonus to this way of doing things is that you only need one phone per school; and it can be paired with a fingerprint scanner to cut down on absenteeism issues. Including the cost of energy, data hosting, devices (over a lifespan of 3 years) the cost is less than $2.50 per class per month.
